key_insight: Short-range order inside grain-boundary complexions fundamentally changes deformation in nanocrystalline Al-Ni: amorphous films favor homogeneous plasticity via shear-transformation zones, while SRO complexions raise strength but trigger stress heterogeneity and shear localization.
engineering_relevance: Complexion engineering can be used to tune the strength-ductility balance in nanocrystalline alloys by controlling whether grain boundaries are amorphous or short-range ordered.
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations to generate complexion states at two Ni levels (2 at.% and 4 at.%).
Molecular dynamics simulations to probe mechanical response after annealing at 913 K vs. 378 K.
Comparison of amorphous intergranular films with semi-amorphous FCC/BCC SRO complexions.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports two composition cases and two annealing conditions, showing a consistent shift from homogeneous plasticity in amorphous complexions to localized shear in SRO complexions.
watch_item: The conclusions are simulation-based for specific Al-Ni compositions and annealing states, so transferability to other alloy systems or processing windows should be validated experimentally.
paper_title: Amorphous vs. Short-Range-Ordered Complexions: Consequences for Grain-Boundary-Mediated Plasticity in Nanocrystalline Al-Ni Alloys
